title: Prominent Southern Baptist Voice Calls For Total Abolition
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States reports on Founders Ministries president Tom Ascol publicly urging Southern Baptists to abandon incremental pro-life legislation and instead demand the total, immediate abolition of abortion, while announcing a forthcoming SBC repentance resolution. The piece situates Ascol's call within a broader push by the Ascol brothers, Oklahoma Southern Baptists, and abolitionist pastors to move the SBC toward abolitionism at the 2021 convention.
audiences: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
SBC denominational leaders and ERLC staff
Church historians tracking evangelical political theology

questions.question: What concrete step is Tom Ascol urging Southern Baptists to take beyond traditional pro-life advocacy?
questions.answer: Ascol is calling Christians, and Southern Baptists specifically, to stop settling for pro-life legislation and instead publicly demand that abortion be abolished totally and immediately.
questions.quote: Tom Ascol, pastor of Grace Baptist Church in Cape Coral, FL and president of Founders Ministries, called Christians and Southern Baptists in particular to move beyond pro-life legislation to call for the total and immediate abolition of abortion.

questions.question: Why does Ascol open his article with the lyrics of Seals and Crofts' 1974 song 'Unborn Child'?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
questions.answer: He uses the song to highlight that a non-Christian rock duo was willing to sacrifice their music careers to denounce abortion the year after Roe, exposing the comparative moral cowardice of Southern Baptists in the same era.
questions.quote: In 1974, one year after the United States Supreme Court created a law to legalize abortion on demand in the Roe v Wade case, the pop rock duo, Jim Seals and Dash Crofts, released a song called “Unborn Child.” That song damaged their music careers and cost them dearly.

questions.question: How does Ascol characterize the Southern Baptist Convention's posture on abortion in the early 1970s?
questions.audience: Church historians tracking evangelical political theology
questions.answer: He argues the SBC was actively promoting legal abortion in cases such as rape, incest, fetal deformity, and threats to the mother's health, even reaffirming that position in 1974 after Roe.
questions.quote: to work for legislation that will allow the possibility of abortion under such conditions as rape, incest, clear evidence of severe fetal deformity, and carefully ascertained evidence of the likelihood of damage to the emotional, mental, and physical health of the mother

questions.question: What does Ascol say Southern Baptists bear responsibility for, and what does he claim is the remedy?
questions.answer: He says Southern Baptists share blame for the deaths of more than sixty million babies due to their failure to speak prophetically, but insists that the blood of Christ can wash away even that stain when sinners repent.
questions.quote: The bloody stain that comes from the more than sixty million babies who have been legally put to death belongs in part to Southern Baptists for their ethical failures to speak clearly, prophetically and consistently on the sanctity of the life of preborn humans. That stain, like all sinful stains, can be removed by the blood of Jesus Christ.

questions.question: What happened to the resolution Ascol references that was originally intended for the 2020 SBC annual meeting?
questions.audience: SBC denominational leaders and ERLC staff
questions.answer: Because the 2020 convention was cancelled, submission of the repentance resolution was deferred, but Ascol insists churches and leaders should not wait to act against abortion in the meantime.
questions.quote: A resolution calling for such repentance was going to be submitted to the 2020 Resolutions Committee of the SBC. Since the convention was cancelled, that will have to wait until next year. But Southern Baptist churches and leaders do not have to wait to take stronger actions to abolish abortion from our nation’s land.

questions.question: How was Derin Stidd's 2019 abolitionist motion treated by SBC leadership and former ERLC staff?
questions.answer: Stidd's motion was killed on a technicality by the SBC executive committee, and former ERLC policy director Matt Hawkins openly mocked its defeat, comparing it to crushing a bug.
questions.quote: The rejection of Stidd’s motion was celebrated by former Ethics and Religious Liberty Commission (the SBC’s political arm) policy director Matt Hawkins who sneered that Stidd’s motion had been “squashed… like the bug it is.”

questions.question: What concrete activist steps does Ascol ask his readers to take?
questions.answer: He instructs them to contact the ERLC and government officials to demand total abolition rather than mere pro-life legislation, and to link up with other Christians in their region who are already engaged in the work.
questions.quote: Contact the Ethics and Religious Liberty Commission and encourage them not simply to work for pro-life legislation, but to start calling for the complete abolition of the murder of unborn children in our nation. Contact federal, state, and local government officials and humbly yet firmly make the same demand. Connect with other Christians in your area who will join you in this effort.

questions.question: Why does Free the States consider Ascol's article a strategically significant development for the abolitionist movement?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
questions.answer: Because Oklahoma Southern Baptists already amended their abortion platform to call for total abolition, and the Ascol brothers plus pastors from multiple states are now positioned to bring abolitionism to the national SBC stage in 2021 alongside state-level abolition bills.
questions.quote: Now the Ascol brothers, Oklahoma Southern Baptist pastors, and abolitionist pastors from numerous states will push for abolitionism at the national SBC in 2021, by which time, hopefully a state will have crossed the finish line and abolished abortion. Bills of total abolition are gaining traction in Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, and other states.

related.title: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
related.reason: Directly referenced in this article as the source of Hawkins' mocking response to Derin Stidd's 2019 abolitionist motion.

related.reason: The November 2019 Oklahoma Baptist resolution explicitly cited here as evidence that Southern Baptist abolitionism is gaining institutional traction.

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: The publication of the very repentance and abolition resolution Ascol previewed for submission after the 2020 convention was cancelled.

related.title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
related.reason: Reports the next chapter of the 2021 SBC effort that Ascol's article was building toward, including objections raised by SBC leaders.

related.title: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
related.reason: Provides the broader historical arc of abolitionism inside the SBC into which Ascol's 2020 call fits.

related.title: Tom Ascol Nominated For Southern Baptist Convention President
related.reason: Follows Tom Ascol's continued rise from prominent abolitionist voice to nominee for SBC president.
